coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from polysaccharides: Chemistry and potential applications
coredata.dc:creator: Emam, Hossam E.
coredata.dc:description: Since the beginning of 21th century, nanoscience and nanotechnology become the most promising topics in various fields, attributing to the superior characters of nanoscaled structures. The conventional quantum dots are substituted with new family of luminescent nanostructures, owing to their interchanged optical properties, low-cost of fabrication, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, ecofriendly, hydrophilicity and superior chemical stability. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were recently investigated for their simple synthesis, bio-consonance, and different revelation applicability. Obeying the green chemistry aspects, this review demonstrates an overview about CQDs generated from polysaccharides in brief, with a background on CQDs discovery, chemical composition, green synthesis via exploitation of different polysaccharides (cellulose, starch, pectin, chitin, etc) as biocompatible/biodegradable abundant biopolymers. Additionally, applications of CQDs originated from polysaccharides in environmental purposes, textiles industry and medical activities were also presented.

CQDs derived from different polysaccharides and their applications.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from polysaccharides: Chemistry and potential applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the beginning of 21th century, nanoscience and nanotechnology become the most promising topics in various fields, attributing to the superior characters of nanoscaled structures. The conventional quantum dots are substituted with new family of luminescent nanostructures, owing to their interchanged optical properties, low-cost of fabrication, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, ecofriendly, hydrophilicity and superior chemical stability. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were recently investigated for their simple synthesis, bio-consonance, and different revelation applicability. Obeying the green chemistry aspects, this review demonstrates an overview about CQDs generated from polysaccharides in brief, with a background on CQDs discovery, chemical composition, green synthesis via exploitation of different polysaccharides (cellulose, starch, pectin, chitin, etc) as biocompatible/biodegradable abundant biopolymers. Additionally, applications of CQDs originated from polysaccharides in environmental purposes, textiles industry and medical activities were also presented.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the recent years, optically active quantum dots have been considerably exploited in various fields, such as, optoelectronics, biotechnology, etc. However, some of such structures were limitedly applied; for instance, ZnS-, CdSe-, and CdS-based semiconducting laborers, or core shell quantum dots (QDs) exhibited a serious toxic impact with low biocompatibility (Chan et al., 2018; Matea et al., 2017). Alternatively, carbon-based/optically active nanostructures, ascribed as carbon dots (CDs or C-dots), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), or polymer quantum dots (PQDs or PDs) were investigated and extensively attracted the attention in different research areas, such as photocatalysis, bioimaging, medical diagnosis, electro- and biosensing, attributing their tunable luminescent characters, low-cost of fabrication, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, ecofriendly, excellent water solubility and chemical stabilization (Das et al., 2018; Das et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2012; Lim et al., 2015; Wang, Lu, et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2015). Recent approaches were extensively considered with preparation and various applications of CQDs in different purposes (Charlton et al., 2023; Hindi et al., 2023; Ji et al., 2023; Magesh et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023).

CQDs with high optical activity were shown with photoluminescence emission. But, the mechanism of photoluminescence emergence is still not affirmed. However, different demonstrated mechanisms were reported in literature (Xingchen et al., 2021), including surface defect states (Ghosh et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2014), quantum size effect (Wang et al., 2019), molecular/molecule like states (Ghosh et al., 2014; Gude et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2014). The surface defect state alludes to the composed surface defect by oxidizing, while, quantum size effects refers to irradiative recombining of the electron–hole pairs (Schneider et al., 2017; Song et al., 2015). In some cases, fluorescence radix of CQDs is attributable to the molecular/molecular- like state, especially in the case of citric acid-derived CQDs (Gude et al., 2016). Such reported mechanisms could explain the different optical characters, like the excitation- and pH-depending properties. Adjusting the excitation wavelength, leads to achieving various photoluminescence emissions, especially when the wavelength of excitation changed (340–480 nm), Liu et al., reported that, maximum wavelength of emission was increased from 450 nm to 550 nm (Liu et al., 2011), owing to the diffusion of CQDs and the surface state (Bhattacharya et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013). CQDs photoluminescence is affected by pH (Zuo et al., 2016) owing to the protonating and deprotonating of functional groups on the surface of CQDs (Dutta Choudhury et al., 2017; Qian et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2016). One of the photoluminescence pH sensing reagents were nucleated with displaying that, the fluorescent intensity is directly proportional to pH (3.5–10) (Shi et al., 2016). Multicolored imaging, pH sensibility, and the mechanism of photoluminescence are adjusted via the excitation/pH-related characters that played the principal role (Jia et al., 2012; Jin et al., 2015; Wang, Rong, et al., 2015).

Photoluminescence sensibility/bioimaging for detection purposes require stable optical signal and a long emissive lifetime. CQDs exhibited high photoluminescence in aqueous media for six hours up to one year (Pei et al., 2015) as CQDs are dispersible stable in aqueous medium and highly saline solutions for prolonged time. CQDs often exhibit with excellent impedance to photo-bleaching as CQDs showed resistance to change in pH over a wide range (3−12) (Wen et al., 2016) whereas, the photo-bleaching impedance is quite important rather than the chemical stabilization, owing to the long observation intervals that were reported in other studies. For instance, different CQDs photoluminescent intensities were slightly diminished after exposure to ultraviolet radiation for 12 h (Wang, Tang, et al., 2015) and one day (Zhuo et al., 2015).

When CQDs are illuminated with the UV irradiation, they emit blue photoluminescence (Chen et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2013). Whereas, biologic analysis utilizes blue-emissive CQDs, that could not be veiled since biological cells also emit blue photoluminescence, that could result in the spectral interfering, so the exploitation of CQDs with long wavelength emission, could conquer this problem. Various reports considered such a problem via surface modification (Zhou et al., 2013), size control (Fang et al., 2012), and pH adjustment (Jia et al., 2012) were applied for ingraining of CQDs with photoluminescence of a longer wavelength. The inter-composition of heteroatoms like nitrogen (Zhang et al., 2012) and oxygen (Vithalani et al., 2020) results in red shifting for the wavelength of emission despite the photoluminescence from bluish to green-yellow. Via the nucleation of CQDs with a long alternative π system, CQDs with reddish photoluminescence were easily nucleated. Nucleation of red-luminescence CQDs with high quantum yield could be executed using p-phenylenediamine (Sun et al., 2018), 1,3-di-hydroxynaphthalene (Wang, Yuan, et al., 2017) and the threefold symmetrical phloroglucinol as origins for ingraining CQDs, Yuan et al. (Yuan et al., 2018) studied the effect of extending the conjugated system under controllable condition for nucleation of strongly photoluminescent CQDs (blue/red) in order to diminish the spectrum interference of the photoluminescent signals and to protect the treated biological tissue from damage under the effect of CQDs with longer wavelengths of emission to share in the photoluminescence mechanism.

The present review is a considerable demonstration of a systematic overview about the CQDs that originated from polysaccharides and their different applications including; soil fertilization, metal detection, the textile industry and biological activity (as summarized in Table 1 ). The current approach also considers highlighting the affinity of different types of polysaccharides for the successive nucleation of CQDs through different synthetic pathways.

Poisoning effects of a certain material is a key parameter of the material denizens in different applications and its environmental manipulation. Recently, the poisoning effects of CQDs were investigated. Whereas, MTT assay (3-(4,5-Dimethyl thiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide assay) is one of the common in vitro toxicity tests that is reported by different researchers. Zhang et al. investigated that CQDs (50–400 μg/mL) in HeLa cells could exhibit frivolous toxicity (Anand et al., 2019; Yang, Cao, et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2014). Sahu et al., were reported that the concentration of CQDs could be accepted in dose as high to be 200 μg/mL for long incubation time (3 days) and show non-poisoning effects to L929 cell lines even at high concentration of 1 mg/mL (Sahu et al., 2012). Zhou et al. reported that the viability in MCF-7 cells was lowered by twenty (Zhou et al., 2015). Toxicity of CQDs is correlated to the surface passivation (Wang, Anilkumar, et al., 2011) and the modulating materials (Wang, Bao, et al., 2011). The obtained data revealed that these type of CQDs were characterized by the low poisoning effects in vitro. Yang et al., were monitored the poisoning impact of CQDs in male CQD-1 mice even at high concentration of 40 mg/kg (CQDs per weight) in accordance to the serum biochemistry and histopathology analysis (Yang, Wang, et al., 2009).

Additionally, in vitro toxicity of CQDs, taking in account the surface charging, mouse fibroblasts NH/3 T3 cell lines (Havrdova et al., 2016) reflected that CQDs with negatively charging (-COOH groups) succeeded in arresting G2/M phase of cell cycling. It could be ascribed that reproduction of CQDs led to higher reactive oxygen species (ROS) index without emanating the cells nuclei. If the surface of CQDs was passivated with positively charged groups, they could show the most toxicity, to be able to get in the nuclei and boost the changing in G0/G1 phases of the cell cycling at low of concentration 100 mg/mL. Cytotoxic profile of CQDs quelled the ingrowth of hepato-cellular carcinoma with IC50 of 0.35 mg/mL, accompanied with eighteen-fold rising in cellular production of ROS (Hsu et al., 2013).

Moreover, CQDs inhibited the ingrowth of liver cancer in a BALB/c mouse paradigm, whereas, such type of CQDs were non-toxic for non-cancerogenic mammalian epithelial cells (MCF-10 A cells), liver cells (FL83B cells), lung cancer (549-cell), breast cancer cells (MDAMB- 231), and cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells) (Li et al., 2014). CQDs solubilized in water were inhibited MCF-7 (IC50 = 0.155 mg/mL) and M DA-MB-231 (IC50 = 0.072 mg/mL) cell growing with lower toxicity to HeLa, renal proximal tubular (LLC-PKI), and MCF- 10 A cells. The inhibition action of CQDs against the tested cancer cells was demonstrated in generation of ROS. CQDs were shown to exhibit a little impacting on thrombin activities by converting fibrinogen to fibrin, so as to pose no harmful effects for blood coagulation. These data confirmed that, CQDs with low toxicity could be ascribed as golden standard in the account of biocompatibility and sustainability. In agreement to the effect of CQDs in air, animals, and aquatic environment (Wang, Xu, et al., 2015), different studies (Borisova et al., 2017) showed low environmental poisoning effects for CQDs when the concentration of CQDs is not so high.

CQDs are essentially characterized with optical properties as photoluminescence emission. However, the fluorescence emergence mechanism is still remaining not detected. There are different reported mechanisms, such as the quantum size effect (Wang et al., 2019), surface defect state (Ghosh et al., 2014) and molecular like states (Ghosh et al., 2014; Gude et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2014). Surface defect state alludes to the formed surface defect by oxidative action, whereas, quantum size effects to size effects on radiation recombining of electron–hole pairs (Schneider et al., 2017; Song et al., 2015). CQDs fluorescence radius in some of cases is attributing to the molecular states, for example those formed from citric acid (Gude et al., 2016). Mechanisms could be explained for the characterized optical characters involving excitation- and pH-attributed characters.

However, adjustancy of the wavelength of excitation, various photoluminescence emission could achieve when the wavelength of excitation was raised from 340 up to 480 nm, Liu et al., investigated that, the maximized emission wavelength could be red shifted from 450 up to 550 nm (Liu et al., 2011), attributing to the dispersion of CQDs and surface states (Bhattacharya et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013). The photoluminescence of CQDs is sensitive to pH (Zuo et al., 2016) due to the protonating and deprotonating of CQDs' surface functional groups (Dutta Choudhury et al., 2017; Qian et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2016). One of the optical pH sensing agents were synthesized, exhibiting with linear relation between the photoluminescent intensity and pH (3.5–10) (Shi et al., 2016). Multi-colored images, pH sensibility, and the photoluminescence mechanism are adjusted via excitation-/pH-correlated characters that play the principal role (Jia et al., 2012; Jin et al., 2015; Wang, Rong, et al., 2015).

Additionally, photoluminescence sensing and imaging for detection applicability requires stable photoluminescence signals and long emissive life times. CQDs emitted strong photoluminescence in aqueous medium for six hours (Su et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2015) or even one year (Pei et al., 2015) as CQDs are shown with high stability when maintained in aqueous solution, even in highly saline solutions for long duration. With dissolution of CQDs in 4 mmole/l sodium chloride in high concentrated hydrogen peroxide (400 μM), Liu et al., observed a significant signal of oxidation to affirm the quite stability of CQDs (Liu et al., 2016). CQDs usually show excellent impedance for photo-bleaching, whereas, CQDs exhibit the affinity for resisting the pH changes in the wide range of 3–12 (Wen et al., 2016), that is, photo-bleaching impedance is more vital than chemical stabilization, owing to the long detection times reported in other analyzing reports. For instance, different CQDs photoluminescent intensities were slightly lowered after exposing to UV-irradiation for twelve hours (Wang, Di, et al., 2015) and for one day (Zhuo et al., 2015).

Up-conversion fluorescence meaning that, the wavelength of emission is shown to be shorter than the wavelength of excitation. Decrement in auto-photoluminescence is attributed to red shifted excitation wavelength and different labels with different emission wavelength and photon tissues permeating enhancement due to up-conversion photoluminescence, making it an principal role for imaging (Cui et al., 2014). CQDs prepared by ultrasonic assisted methodology exhibits the character of up-conversion photoluminescence (Li, He, Liu, Huang, et al., 2011; Li, He, Liu, Yu, et al., 2011; Su et al., 2020). CQDs synthesized from glucose and NH4OH showed up-conversion emission in the wavelength of 300–600 nm with the excitation wavelength up to 650–1000 nm (Su et al., 2020). Two photon absorption might emit up-conversion photoluminescence (Vithalani et al., 2020). Fig. 1 represents a comparable overview for the optical properties of CQDs derived from different polysaccharides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic approaches for nucleation of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, synthesis methodologies of CQDs were investigated to be adapted in accordance to the exploited carbon origin. CQDs are implanted via one of two synthetic techniques, Top-down and Bottom-up. Whereas, top-down proceeded via breaking down the bulk carbon compounds into nanomaterials via the physical cleaving or by the chemical degradation (oxidation). However, the bottom-up includes a chemical reaction that exploits various carbon-based resources (De, 2019; Jamaludin et al., 2019). Top-down approaches were basically applied in the first reported researches for implantation of CQDs, in which CQDs were nucleated from extraction and rupturing of carbon compounds as carbon nanotubes and graphite (Pan et al., 2020).

In the top-down technique, the most exploited synthetic methodologies are chemical oxidation, arc discharge, electrochemical and laser ablation (De, 2019). These techniques generate CQDs with various QY (quantum yields) and defined optical activity. Nevertheless, these methodologies require acidic medium, high energy and elevated temperature, making the applied processes dangerous, unstable, and not ecofriendly; in addition to, the generated CQDs do not show good characters of size distribution (De, 2019; Nuri et al., 2019). However, bottom-up approach proceeds for implantation of CQDs from the organic resources via the polymerization and carbonization reactions. Some of the mostly applicable synthetic techniques are direct heating, ultrasound, microwave, pyrolysis and hydrothermal techniques (De, 2019; Jamaludin et al., 2019). These methodologies allow CQDs generation with well-defined morphological features, controllable size and good PL (photoluminescent) properties (Jamaludin et al., 2019). Bottom-up synthetic approach was reported to be cost effective and time saving, since the effect of microwave irradiation, ultrasonic waves, temperature, and pressure are superiorly efficient to give enough amount of energy for successive implantation of CQDs (Pan et al., 2020). In recent years, new reports tend to investigate affinity of bottom-up approach since it exploits natural resources or simple chemicals without of hazards chemical reagents (Pan et al., 2020).

Numerous researchers studied the development and exploitation of green synthesis which is an ecofriendly methodology (Hoan et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019). Meaning that, synthetic methodologies like microwave and hydrothermal conditions for implantation of CQDs were enhanced to achieve lower costed results with time saving, using lesser reactants, lower energy consumption with manufacturing in a large scale (Hoan et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

These alternative green synthetic techniques exploit various carbon-based precursors such as plants, enzymes, microorganisms, organic wastes, and natural products (Huang et al., 2019; Jamaludin et al., 2019; Ng, 2019; Sargin et al., 2019). It is essential to select the compatible carbon-based resources that are low in cost and highly abundant, as well as affirming superior CQDs characters. However, natural products, biomolecules, and organic wastes are characterized by high abundance and capability to produce non-toxicity, biocompatibility of CQDs with controllable size-distribution, and optical properties (Fan et al., 2020).

In especial, polysaccharides are the most exploitable organic compounds for living organisms, so as they provide different vital actions and in terms of obtained volume, they demonstrate approximately seventy five percentage of the annually regenerated biomolecules (Chida & Sato, 2012). Only 4 % of these amounts is exploited by human beings, whereas, the natural products, principally as food materials but also as resources for heat, paper, and lumber (Lindhauer, 1994). Polysaccharides are considered as a most common raw materials in biorefinery of sustainable chemical reagents, promoting a transition from the application of fossil-based and limited precursors to a regenerated biomolecular-based chemical industrialization (Sheldon, 2018). Meaning that, it could reveal that, CQDs implantation from polysaccharides, to purpose their future manufacturing processes in accordance to green chemistry concepts (Mann et al., 2007). From these point, some of the most applicable types of polysaccharides are hemicellulose, cellulose, starch, chitin, pectin..etc.. (Ramesh & Tharanathan, 2003).

Different approaches were studied for the exploitation of polysaccharides for implantation of CQDs. Starch as one of the polysaccharides with as semi-crystalline chains with 5–40 μm diameter, is inter-composed of two ∝ − glucose-based polymers, named as amylose as a linear polymer and amylopectin with a branched chain structural form (Chatterjee et al., 2015; Moad, 2011). Starch is generally represented in rhizomes, tubers and seeds for plants that are exploited for energy storing, whereas, humans exploit it for energy supplying (Chatterjee et al., 2015; Moad, 2011). Several studies were considered with synthesis of CQDs from different starch-containing resources like potato, yam, rice, cassava, maize..etc.… Meanwhile, Adams et al. (Adams & Fagbenro-Owoseni, 2017), applied microwave assisted technique to ingrain CQDs without surface decoration, from the as-mentioned starch-based precursors (10–15.4 nm), in which by varying the reaction duration, florescence color was tunable from blue, green to yellow. In another study, Qiang et al. (Qiang et al., 2019) were applied the ultrasonic technique to obtain green CQDs (3–5 nm) from potato starch that is known as enriched resource with oxygen-based functional groups. Whereas, the implanted CQDs were exploited for the detection of zinc ions after the emission at different wavelengths (λ), with unique excitation to show high pH sensitivity. Dong et al. (Dong et al., 2020), applied the solvothermal methodology and to produce phosphorus (P) & nitrogen (N) doped CQDs (N-P-CQDs) of from maize starch (2.5 nm) with blue photoluminescence and high quantum yield (76.5 %). Good photoluminescence of CQDs make it highly applicable for detection of ferric ions.

Monosaccharides such as pentoses & hexose or disaccharides such as lactose, sucrose, and maltose (Chatterjee et al., 2015). Such types of saccharides are existed in many types of foods and natural products such as milk, honey, fruit, vegetables, etc., to act the key role in the provision of energy for consumption, as well as enhancement of the texture of prepared foods, flavor, and preservation (Bertoft, 2017). Various researching reports were considered with the exploitation of sugars sources (e.g. sucrose, glucose, red cabbage, etc.….) for implantation of CQDs. Chen et al., (Chen et al., 2018) were applied the ultrasonic-assisted technique for implantation of CQDs (4 nm) from glucose as enriched source of hydroxyl groups, with maximized wavelength of emission (516 nm). Whereas, CQDs were sequentially applied for the detection of gallic acid (in tea and orange juice). Zhang et al., (Zhang et al., 2019) also reported that, the chemical treatment of sucrose resulted in successive ingraining of hydrophilic/negative charged N-P-CQDs (1.765–6.25 nm) as blue photoluminescent dots with quantum yield of 6.9 % through different excitation wavelength ranged in 300 and 500 nm.

Similarly, Sharma et al., (Sharma et al., 2020) applied the hydrothermal technique in order to obtain CQDs (2–6 nm) from red cabbage with (QY 8.3 %), to show antioxidizing action and high photostability with storage for one day, without topographical changing in neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells, consequently, the prepared CQDs were exploited for labeling the foodborne and ink manufacturing. Whereas, authors suggested that, the demonstrated technique could be expressed as promising methodology for sensing purposes.

Another approach studied the effect of chemical medication for starch with glucose to be consequently exploited for synthesis of CQDs to be applied as viricidal/anticancer reagents (Emam et al., 2023). The demonstrated results revealed that, with alkaline dissolution, carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) were synthesized from glucose (15.5 ± 6.8 nm), whereas, carbogenic nanodots (CgNDs) nucleated from starch (4.8 ± 1.8 nm), and graphitic nanodots (GNDs) were nucleated from glucose-functionalized starch (3.1 ± 1.3 nm).

Cellulose macromolecule is identified as a rigid, highly crystalline, water insoluble polymer; is constituted of linear polymeric chains of unbranched β-glucose (40–60 %). Cellulose is mainly existed in the woody biomasses and is the primary constituent that is responsible for the mechanical strengthening of the cell wall in the plants (Atalla, 1990; Chatterjee et al., 2015). Various reports were studied the affinity of different cellulosic resources for CQDs implantation (like cotton, coconut husk and pine fruit). Shahba et al., (Shahba & Sabet, 2020) were succeeded in implantation of CQDs (emission wavelength of 503 nm) under the hydrothermal conditions using the pine fruit that is mainly composed of 89 % cellulose; the authors also mentioned that, synthesis of carbon nanofibers could be proceeded using metals (eg. iron) that are existed in pine fruit. The photoluminescence intensity of the generated CQDs was increased (by 40 %) via the modification of surface with PVP (polyvinylpyrrolidone), PEG (polyethylene glycol), AcAc (acetylacetone), and EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid). Additionally, CQDs exhibited with a photocatalytic capacity via the degradation of various types of dyes, whereas, the absorptivity was100% for lead and cadmium ions.

In another approach, Zou et al., (Zou et al., 2020) performed the solvothermal technique with exploitation of cotton as a source of cellulose to implant blue CQDs (maximum emission wavelength of 360 nm). In an acidic media, most of the carboxyl and hydroxyl on the surface of the CQDs that were generated from cellulose, could undergo hydrogen bonding, which leads to the aggregation of the generated CQDs, to increase the particle size and decrease the photoluminescence intensity. Additionally, CQDs could be successfully uploaded within fiber matrix to be applicable for detection of mercury ions. Chunduri et al. (Chunduri et al., 2016) were exploited the coconut husk for obtaining blue CQDs (1–8 nm), under hydrothermal conditions. CQDs were not affected with the excitation wavelength, so as the particles were well-dispersed, represented with photoluminescence with linear correlation with pH, and characterized with antioxidizing action.

Another study (Mogharbel et al., 2023) represented for a comparable study for approving the affinity of CQDs clustered from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) under hydrothermal conditions (HT) versus that synthesized under infrared assisted method (IR), whereas, the size average for CQDs-HT & CQDs-IR was estimated to be 7.8 & 9.8 nm, respectively (Fig. 2a &b).

In more recent report, CQDs were successfully implanted from carboxymethyl cellulose (Ahmed et al., 2022), Whereas, CQDs were implanted from the alkali-treated carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) with the hydrothermal process. The nucleated CQDs with particle size 6.8 nm (Fig. 2c) were immobilized within Cu based metal organic frame work (Cu-benzene tricarboxylic acid, Cu-BTC), to prepare composite of CQDs@Cu-BTC. Moreover, In another report (Ahmed et al., 2021), a unique technique was demonstrated for ingraining of CQDs with particle size of 8.0 nm form carboxymethyl cellulose through the infrared-assisted method.

Moreover, hemicellulose as one of the branched polymeric structures, is composed of 2 hexoses and 2 pentoses units, and is amorphous polymer that could be easily dissolved into its monomeric units (Chatterjee et al., 2015). In food industry, hemicellulose is widely applied in cereal grains (Dhingra et al., 2012). Several studies were reported the exploitation of various sources of hemicellulose (such as, hemicellulose isolated from industrial wastes, oil palm, sorghum, millet, fruit bunch, wheat and rice) for implanting CQDs. Jiang et al., (Jiang et al., 2020) were studied the application of solvothermal method for exploitation of industrial hemicellulose for ingraining of blue N-CQDs (N doped CQDs, 2–8 nm), with quantum yield of 23.45 %. The as-produced CQDs were exhibited with optical stability with storage for one month, to be sequentially diminished with 85 % of photoluminescence intensity, while pH did not trigger a big changing in CQDs photoluminescence. These good photoluminescence characters resulted in successive application of CQDs as markers for estimation the concentration of silver ions and L-Cysteine.

Similarly, Mahat and Shamsudin (Mahat & Shamsudin, 2020) were studied the application of hydrothermal technique for exploitation of oil palm of empty fruit bunch as a source of hemicellulose in order to ingrain blue CQDs (2–5 nm) with maximum wavelength of emission (450 nm). Photoluminescence intensity was shown to be increased by increment of the concentration of CQDs; meaning that, at low concentration, the interactions among the different polar groups may be decreased. Chaudhary et al. (Chaudhary et al., 2016) were applied the thermal degradation technique in nitrogen atmosphere, using sorghum, millet, wheat and rice, as sources of hemicellulose, in order to ingrain CQDs (5–10 nm) with quantum yield of 30 %, 17 %, 33 % and 16 %, respectively; whereas, increment in pH resulted in enhancement of photoluminescence intensity. Authors proposed that pH could affect the dynamic equilibrium among the excitons of defect-charged and defect-bound excitons.

Pectin as one of the hydrophilic polysaccharides which is existed in the fiber within the 1° cell wall and the intracellular layers for the cells of plants. Additionally, pectin is existed within citrus fruits (0.5 % and 3.5 %); it is also found in citrus peels, in food as a viscous laborer in beverage and soft drink (Trugo & Finglas, 2003). Different approaches were studied the synthesis of CQDs from different pectin resources (such as orange peel, citrus peel and quince fruit powder). In recent study, Ayaz Ahmed et al., (Ahmed & Veerappan, 2016) were used citrus peel as a pectin precursor for synthesis of blue CQDs (10–15 nm) under hydrothermal conditions, whereas, the produced CQDs were applied for the detection of Ag ions without the requirement of irradiation or thermal energy or any additional reducing reagents. Photoluminescence emission for CQDs in the presence of Ag ions was originally converted from blue through cyan to green and finally to colorless.

Whereas, Ramezani et al., (Ramezani et al., 2018) were studied the affinity of quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) powder as a source of pectin in order to obtain blue CQDs (4.85 nm) using microwave-assisted technique, whereas, the ingrained CQDs were applied in detection of As3+ ions. However, the researches were mentioned that, temperature id the most important key parameter that mainly affecting on the photoluminescence of CQDs. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2020) studied the compatibility of orange peel pectin for synthesis of blue CQDs (2.34–2.99 nm) under the hydrothermal conditions with maximum wavelength of emission (440 nm), whereas CQDs were chemically modified via treatment with EDTA, to be sequentially applied in effective detection of Cr4+ ions.

Chitin as one of the most naturally abundant type of polysaccharides, is existed in 2 crystalline forms of alpha-chitin and beta-chitin, whereas there is a third form known as gamma-chitin that is a combination of alpha- and beta- forms. Chitin is commonly originated in the lower organisms (like; crustaceans, insects and fungi) and not in mammals. Previously, it was suggested that the insect fibers is mainly composed of chitin, but, it was reported that it represents only a percent of 1 % - 8 % (Sudha et al., 2014). Numerous reports were studied the superiority of different chitin resources for nucleation of CQDs, such like; chitosan and prawn shell powder (Wang, Li, et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2019). In recent years, Zhao et al. (Zhao et al., 2019) were applied a one-step hydrothermal technique for exploitation of chitosan as N doped CQDs precursor (2 nm & blue photoluminescent emission), whereas, the as-ingrained CQDs were exhibited with high water solubility, excitation-correlated behavior with good quantitative estimation of ferric cations in aquatic media.

Similarly, Wang et al., (Wang, Li, et al., 2016) approved the efficiency of chitosan to generate multicolor nitrogen containing CQDs (3.8 nm) with quantum yield of 31.8 %, under hydrothermal conditions. The as-synthesized CQDs were shown with high potentiality for detection of heavy metals, particularly for Hg2+; this potentiality was exploited for developing a smart phone industry in order to make the manufactured phone capable for identifying the concentrations of Hg2+, according to the change in sample color. Gedda et al., (Gedda et al., 2016) were synthesized blue CQDs (4 nm) under the treatment with green chemical conditions from prawn shell waste as a source of chitin, with photoluminescence that is pH dependent, with low quantum yield (9 %). During the detection of Cu2+ ions in saline water, CQDs were not affected by the changing of ionic strength or storage duration.

Different polysaccharides showed high affinity for nucleation of CQDs through simple methods, whereas, the hydrothermal technique as the most popular method of preparation. In accordance to literature, CQDs based on polysaccharides with different average sizes and optical properties. Photoluminescence color for CQDs in most of polysaccharides groups was mainly blue in color (Sharma et al., 2020). Additionally, it could declare that, the small molecules resulted in easier CQDs nucleation, whereas, the large carbon molecules need an extra energy for activation that must be controlled in the operational parameters through the implantation processes; accordingly, sugars as CQDs precursors require lesser energy of activation rather than polysaccharides for successive ingraining of CQDs (Ng, 2019). Besides, the process for doping with nitrogen or phosphorous is commonly reflected in the enhancement of optical properties, that is a key parameter for the development of highly sensitive CQDs sensing laborers. CQDs based on polysaccharides represent superior advantages, as one of the most relevant advantages is their easy production from biomass resources, to be reflected in its cost effectiveness.

In recent approach, the effect of condensation reaction for interaction of chitosan with glucose was demonstrated for studying the affinity of chitosan macromolecules as biopolymer resource of CQDs. Chitosan conjugated glucose was firstly synthesized via the Maillard reaction, to consequently applied for ingraining of CQDs (Ahmed et al., 2023). While, CQDs that were nucleated from chitosan under acidic conditions exhibited with particle size of 10.6 nm & basic, whereas, that nucleated under basic conditions was characterized with smaller size of 5.7 nm (Fig. 2i & j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pullulan, carrageenan and cyclodextrin as CQDs-generator
ce:section.ce:para: β-Cyclodextrin (CD) was recently exploited as a generator of lipophobic polymer dots (PDs). The data revealed that, PDs prepared at 190 °C under the basic conditions (5.1 nm) was nucleated with rationally bigger size compared to that clustered at acidic conditions with size of 2.9 nm (Almahri et al., 2023). Another research (Emam & Ahmed, 2021) focused on approving the efficiency of pullulan macromolecule versus carrageenan macromolecule as OH- and OH-/SO3-based polysaccharides, respectively, in nucleation of CQDs. The study showed the effect of functional groups on the decoration and in turn the applicability of the ingrained CQDs, whereas, CQDs were nucleated with mean size of 2.1 nm from both of pullulan and carrageenan (Fig. 2d &e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CQDs derived from polysaccharides

ce:section.ce:para: Fertilizers in nano-dimensions were advantageously characterized with minimum negative impacts in needing excessive amounts with diminishing the number of cycles for applications. It was recently reported that (Ahmed et al., 2022), CQDs immobilized within the matrix of Cu-metal organic framework (Cu-benzene tricarboxylic acid, Cu-BTC) showed high affinity as a fertilizer for sun flowers. The prepared CQDs and CQDs@Cu-BTC were exploited for nutrition of sun flowers. The data revealed that, chlorophyll a and carotenoids contents as key parameters of monitoring the plant rate of growth, were estimated to be 0.46 & 0.49 after fertilizing with CQDs and 0.35 & 0.36 mg/g after fertilization with CQDs@Cu-BTC`.

The existence of heavy metals in the soil or in aquatic environment could not be biodegraded with microorganisms (Fan et al., 2020). Whereas, they could be absorbed via the livestock and plants, so as they could be entered the feeding chain to cause a serious health problems (Gori et al., 2019). Resulting in the priority for estimating the concentration of heavy metals especially in mind a food quality. Heavy metals that were most frequently estimated in food are Cu+2, Hg+2 and Fe+3. Till now, many techniques were established for detection of heavy metals in food such like (atomic absorption spectrometry, AAS and the inductively coupled plasma spectrometry). Despite of the high selectivity of the as-mentioned techniques, but, they usually require tedious operations and expensive instrumental tools (Zhao et al., 2018).

The mechanism of detection for heavy metals could be illustrated based on the truth that the interaction of nano-dimensional objects with metals, resulting in quenching or enhancing of the photoluminescence spectral data, with excitation up to 340 nm (Fig. 3 ) (Li & Li, 2020; Varun & Daniel, 2018). Photoluminescent methodologies were attracted a great attention for the detection of heavy metals, owing to their advantages of cost effectiveness, time saving, simplicity, and real-time following up. Recently, some reports were considered with studying the effective exploitation of CQDs for detection of heavy metals. Copper ions as a co-factor of different enzymes, act a vital role in pathological and physiological fields. High contents of copper ions in the human body can result in simulation of different diseases (such as Parkinson's disease, Wilson's disease and prion disease) (Wang, Zhang, et al., 2016). Therefore, it is important to investigate a highly efficient/selective strategy for estimating of copper cations. Zhao et al. (Zhao et al., 2018) were studied the successive synthesis of CQDs by utilizing citric acid as CQDs-generator, and performed a for determination of the dispersion of copper ions in fruit juice (Zhao et al., 2018). The mechanism of detection was identified as a charge transferring with dynamic quenching, resulting in interaction between copper ions and the decorative groups within CQDs surface. Whereas, the detection limit for copper ions were quite low with range of 5–190 nM (Murugan et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018).

Mercury cations can be accumulated in the animals and marine plants, attributing to the environmental pollution (Hua et al., 2017). Whereas, mercury cations cause cognitive dysfunctionality, kidney impairing, and cardiovascular diseases through the agglomeration in the body of human beings even at the very low concentrations (Gao et al., 2018). He et al., (He et al., 2020) studied the affinity of citric acid for nucleation of N-CQDs to be consequently applicable in the detection of mercury cations in sea food. The detection mechanism explained according to electron transferring, resulting in the chemical interaction between mercury cations and the functional groups of the exploited CQDs, to change the electron cloud densities of the functional groups, resulting in the decrement of energy traps. LOD for mercury cations using CQDs metal markers were 3,13.48,37, and 150 nM (Chatzimitakos et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2020; He et al., 2020; Hua et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2019). Recently, Emam et al., synthesized CQDs from guar gum by hydrothermal technique, with size distribution of 1.5–6.5 nm (Fig. 3f, g & h) and showed sensitivity towards Hg2+ and Zn2+ (Ahmed & Emam, 2020). The quenching of fluorescence was observed depending on the metal ion concentration. The detection limit was in the range of 0–85 mM of Mn+ and the study showed the high sensitivity of fluorescent CQDs to metal at low metal concentration.

It was reported that (Ahmed et al., 2021), CQDs can be applied for the industrialization of biocidal/fluorescent cotton fabrics. CQDs were impregnated within both of native & cationized cotton fabrics for preparation of CQDs@cotton and CQDs@cationized cotton. The photoluminescent emission spectrum for CQDs showed two spectral bands at 415–445 nm. However, the photoluminescent emmision intensity was lowered from 741 to 287 after ten washings.

CQDs were superiorly applied for designing of the military fabrics as photoluminescent/UV-resistant cotton fabrics with antimicrobial action. In such study, CQDs were ingrained from pyrimidine based heterocycle compound (Emam et al., 2021). The ingrained CQDs were impregnated within fabrics/cationized fabrics in order to obtain CQDs@cotton and CQDs@Q-cotton fabrics. The estimated photoluminescent intensity, UV-blocking and antimicrobial activity for CQDs@Q-cotton are exhibited with higher affinity rather than CQDs@cotton. In addition to that, CQDs@Q-cotton is shown with excellent durability even after five washing cycles, whereas, the yellowness index was insignificantly decreased from 26.5 to 20.3, whereas, the photoluminescence intensity was decreased from 540 nm to 340 nm. UPF rating (UV protection factor) was also lowered from very good to good.

A successive implantation of CQDs ingrained from CMC showed pathogenic reduction of 96 % for E. coli and 82 % for C. albicans. However, after impregnation of the as-obtained CQDs within cotton fabrics to prepare CQDs@cationized cotton, it acquired the prepared fabrics excellent antimicrobial action as the prepared fabrics displayed superior microbicidal action, with good durability even after 10 washing cycles, as the diameter of inhibition zone was measured to be diminished from 21.0 mm to 14.0 mm against E. coli (Ahmed et al., 2021). While, in another report, CQDs that were superiorly acquired the treated cotton fabrics excellent microbial inhibitions against C. albicans, E. coli and S. aureus, whereas, the microbial inhibition percent was evaluated to be 67 %, 63 % and 68 %, respectively (Emam et al., 2021).

The compatibility of chitosan against chitosan conjugated glucose as precursor for CQDs to be applicable in turn as anticancer and antiviral was presented (Ahmed et al., 2023). The synthesized CQDs were examined against the lung cancer (NSCLC, A549) and the IC50 was ranged in 282.4–165.4 μg/mL. While, the antiviral activity of CQDs was monitored contra the Corona Virus (series of 229E) and the estimated IC50 was 43.6–19.3 mg/mL. CQDs nucleated from chitosan conjugated glucose exhibited much higher antitumor and viricidal actions comparing with that ingrained from the original chitosan. So, it could declare that, conjugation of chitosan with glucose via the Maillard reaction acted in improving of the biological activity of the obtained CQDs to be effectively exploited as anticancer & antiviral reagents.

The results of another approach (Emam et al., 2023) revealed that, against Corona Virus (series, 229E), the antiviral activity of CNPs, CgNDs and GNDs was approved, whereas, IC50 was evaluated to be 76.7 (CNPs), 61.2 (CgNDs) and 29.6 mg/mL (GNDs) for the prepared nanostructures. CNPs, CgNDs and GNDs were also examined contra the lung cancer (NSCLC, A549) and the measured IC50 was 475.9 (CNPs), 356.5 (CgNDs) and 220.3 μg/mL (GNDs).

On the other hand, another approach showed that (Mogharbel et al., 2023), CQDs prepared under hydrothermal conditions exhibited higher anti-proliferation against the Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HepG2) comparing with the CQDs prepared under the effect of infra-red irradiation. Treatment with 1000 μg/mL of CQDs prepared under infrared & CQDs under hydrothermal effect exhibited 78 & 90 % mortal percentage of the cancer cells, respectively. The clustered CQDs characterized with excellent antimicrobial action and MIC from CQDs prepared under infra-red and hydrothermal conditions was ranged in 100–350 μg/mL and 300–400 μg/mL against different pathogens, respectively. So, as it was declared that, hydrothermal technique showed superiority over infrared for the nucleation of CQDs with higher anticancer and antimicrobial action. Moreover, pullulan showed higher efficiency over carrageenan for in graining CQDs with better anticancer action. While, the antiviral estimation potency confirmed the priority of carrageenan against pullulan in generation of CQDs as antiviral reagent (Emam & Ahmed, 2021).

Hydrogels based on polysaccharides and CQDs were designed to be applied in drug delivery. The targeted hydrogels were synthesized via a chemical modification of hyaluronic acid with CQDs (Goh et al., 2012). In vivo assessment affirmed the successive delivery of hyaluronic acid@CQDs hydrogel to the receptors of hyaluronic acid within liver cells for exploiting the nucleated CQDs as bioimaging agent. The affinity of CQDs@chitosan hydrogel in drug delivery for 5-Fluorouracil as anticancer drug was also approved (Sachdev et al., 2016). Drug coated with CQDs@chitosan hydrogel were decreased the cell viability percent evaluated via MTT assay for A549 and NIH 3 T3 cells after 48 h. Delivery of doxorubicin (Dox) as anticancer laborer was realized via the release of the drug in acidic environment through the chemical modification of CQDs@DNA hydrogel (Singh et al., 2017). Moreover, PEG@chitosan@CQDs pH-sensitive hydrogel with optical sensibility was successfully prepared (Wang, Tang, et al., 2015). The hybrid hydrogel was shown with near IR dual response delivery for hydrophobic anticancer laborer. The utilization of CQDs@polymer were found to exhibit multi-stimulated-response for pH, organic molecules, temperature and inorganic ions (Yin et al., 2013).

The current review represents an overview about the affinity of different polysaccharides (e.g. cellulose, starch, pectin, chitin, etc) for successive ingraining of CQDs as cost effective and green synthetic pathway. Synthetic methods, optical properties and cytotoxicity of CQDs were also highlighted and considered. The applicability of CQDs-based polysaccharides in soil fertilization, metal detection, textile finishing and biological activities are summarized. The recent challenges and future perspectives could be suggested in order to open up investigative angles for exploiting of such green reports for CQDs fabrication with promising photoluminescent properties to be widening their applicability scopes.
